label: APSGN — Strep Eradication
drug: Benzathine Penicillin G · IM · Single dose
explanation: 22 kg < 27 kg → 600,000 U IM. The boundary is exactly 27 kg.
trap: <27 kg = 600K · ≥27 kg = 1.2M. One of the most tested dose thresholds in KUHS.

label: APSGN — Strep Eradication
drug: Benzathine Penicillin G · IM · Single dose
explanation: 31 kg > 27 kg → 1.2 million U IM. Trap: students often remember only one value.
trap: Same threshold question — heavier child. ≥27 kg = 1.2M.

label: Nephrotic Syndrome — First episode
explanation: NS first episode phase 1: 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) × 6 weeks.
trap: 1 mg/kg is the phase 2 alternate-day dose. Don't swap the phases.

label: Nephrotic Syndrome — Apply cap
drug: Prednisolone · PO · What dose do you give?
explanation: 2 mg/kg × 42 kg = 84 mg but max cap = 60 mg/day. Always apply the ceiling.
trap: Max cap = 60 mg/day regardless of weight. 84 mg = -1 mark.

label: Nephrotic Syndrome — Phase 2 weeks 7–12
explanation: NS phase 2: 1.5 mg/kg on alternate days × 6 weeks. Total course = 12 weeks for responders.
trap: Phase 2 = 1.5 mg/kg alternate days. Not 1 mg/kg, not 2 mg/kg.

label: Nephrotic Syndrome — Hypovolaemia
explanation: NS hypovolaemia: 25% albumin 0.5–1.0 g/kg IV over 1–2 hours. Follow with loop diuretic.
trap: For HYPOVOLAEMIA only — not for routine oedema management.

label: AGN — Fluid Overload
explanation: Furosemide 1–2 mg/kg IV for fluid overload in AGN. NOT for hypertension.
trap: Furosemide = fluid overload. Nifedipine = HTN. These are not interchangeable.

label: Rickets — Hypocalcaemic Seizure
explanation: Hypocalcaemic seizures: Ca gluconate 10% at 1–2 mL/kg slowly over 10 min with ECG monitoring.
trap: SLOW infusion with cardiac monitoring. Rapid bolus → bradycardia / cardiac arrest.

label: Congenital Hypothyroidism
explanation: L-thyroxine 10–15 mcg/kg/day PO. Start within 2 weeks of life for normal neurodevelopment.
trap: Start WITHIN 2 WEEKS of life. Delay = irreversible intellectual disability.

label: Neonatal Hypoglycaemia — Symptomatic
explanation: Symptomatic neonatal hypoglycaemia or glucose <25 mg/dL: D10 2 mL/kg IV bolus.
trap: Feed FIRST if asymptomatic. IV D10 only if symptomatic or glucose <25 mg/dL.

label: Nutritional Rickets — Treatment
explanation: Treatment dose: Vit D3 2000–4000 IU/day for 6–12 weeks. 400 IU/day = prevention only.
trap: Treatment ≠ prevention. 400 IU = prevention. 2000–4000 = treatment. Don't confuse.

label: Nutritional Rickets — Stoss
explanation: Stoss therapy: 150,000–300,000 IU oral single dose. Repeat at 3 months if needed.
trap: Stoss = 150K–300K IU. 50K is insufficient. 600K is excessive.

label: Rickets — alongside Vit D
explanation: Elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day must accompany Vit D. Never omit this.
trap: Vitamin D alone is NOT sufficient. Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside it.

label: DKA — After 1h fluids · start insulin
explanation: DKA insulin: 0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr IV continuous infusion. NEVER bolus. Always start after fluids.
trap: NEVER bolus insulin in paediatric DKA. Continuous infusion only, started after 1–2 h fluids.

label: DKA — K⁺ = 3.0 mEq/L
explanation: K⁺ < 3.3 mEq/L: HOLD insulin. Correct potassium first. Insulin + hypokalaemia = fatal arrhythmia.
trap: K⁺ <3.3 → Hold insulin. The single most dangerous DKA management error.

label: DKA — Initial Resuscitation
explanation: DKA initial fluid: 0.9% NS 10–20 mL/kg over 1–2 h. Replace total deficit over 48 h, not 24 h.
trap: Replace DKA deficit over 48 h NOT 24 h. Rapid replacement → cerebral oedema.

label: DKA — Cerebral Oedema
explanation: Cerebral oedema in DKA: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or 3% NaCl 5–10 mL/kg. Reduce fluids + elevate head.
trap: Both mannitol AND 3% saline are valid options. Must also reduce fluid rate.

label: Diarrhoea — Some Dehydration · Plan B
explanation: Plan B (some dehydration, 3–9%): ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours in clinic.
trap: Plan B = 75 mL/kg ORS. Plan C = IV RL bolus. These two are classic exam swap traps.

label: Severe Dehydration — Plan C · Infant
explanation: Infant <12 months: RL 30 mL/kg over 1 HOUR. (Child 1–5 yr: same volume over 30 min.)
trap: Age matters: <12 mo = 1 hour. 1–5 yr = 30 min. Most common Plan C error.

label: Severe Dehydration — Plan C · Child
explanation: Child 1–5 years: RL 30 mL/kg over 30 MIN. Then 70 mL/kg over next 5 hours.
trap: Age matters: 1–5 yr = 30 min. <12 mo = 1 hour. Opposite of infant — common trap.

label: Acute Diarrhoea — Zinc
explanation: <6 months: Zinc 10 mg/day × 10–14 days. ≥6 months: 20 mg/day.
trap: Age cutoff at 6 months. <6 mo = 10 mg. ≥6 mo = 20 mg. Frequently tested in MCQ.

label: Acute Diarrhoea — Zinc
explanation: ≥6 months: Zinc 20 mg/day × 10–14 days. Reduces duration and severity.
trap: ≥6 mo = 20 mg. <6 mo = 10 mg. Opposite side of the same MCQ trap.

label: APSGN — Acute HTN
explanation: First-line acute HTN in AGN: Nifedipine (CCB). ACE-i contraindicated — worsen hyperkalaemia + AKI.
trap: ACEi in acute AGN = instant marks off. Answer = Nifedipine. Furosemide = fluid overload only.

label: HUS — STEC E. coli confirmed
explanation: Antibiotics CONTRAINDICATED in STEC-HUS — increase Shiga toxin release, worsening the syndrome.
trap: No antibiotics in STEC-HUS. Exception: pneumococcal HUS (antibiotics ARE indicated).

label: Portal HTN — Variceal Bleed
explanation: Target Hb 7–8 g/dL only. Over-transfusion raises portal pressure and dramatically increases rebleeding.
trap: Over-transfusion INCREASES portal pressure. Target 7–8, NOT 10–12.

label: UTI — Cystitis duration
explanation: Uncomplicated cystitis: 3–5 days. Pyelonephritis: 10–14 days. Too short = recurrence; too long = resistance.
trap: Cystitis = 3–5 days. Pyelonephritis = 10–14 days. Duration trap is commonly tested.

label: Pyelonephritis — Infant <2mo
explanation: Infants <2 months: Ampicillin + Gentamicin or Ampicillin + Cefotaxime. Ceftriaxone avoided due to bilirubin displacement.
trap: Ceftriaxone CONTRAINDICATED in neonates. Use Ampicillin + Gentamicin.

label: VUR — Prophylaxis
explanation: VUR prophylaxis: Cephalexin 10–15 mg/kg at night. Safe in infants. Alternative: Cotrimoxazole 2 mg/kg TMP.
trap: Cephalexin 10–15 mg/kg at night. Cotrimoxazole contraindicated <2 months.

label: CAH — Adrenal crisis
explanation: CAH crisis: Hydrocortisone 50–100 mg/m²/day IV Q6H for 24–48h. Maintenance = 10–15 mg/m²/day PO.
trap: Stress dose is 5–10× higher than maintenance. Do NOT confuse them.

label: Wilson Disease — Chelation
explanation: D-Penicillamine 20 mg/kg/day (max 1–2 g/day). MUST give pyridoxine 25–50 mg/day alongside.
trap: Always give PYRIDOXINE with penicillamine — anti-pyridoxine effect causes neuropathy.

label: Paracetamol Poisoning — NAC
explanation: NAC IV: 150 mg/kg (1h) + 50 mg/kg (4h) + 100 mg/kg (16h) = 300 mg/kg total.
trap: IV total = 300 mg/kg. Oral total = 1330 mg/kg. Different regimens.

label: SAM — Phase 1 stabilization
explanation: SAM Phase 1: F-75 at 130 mL/kg/day divided q2h. Phase 2: F-100 at 150–220 kcal/kg/day.
trap: Phase 1 = F-75 low calorie. Phase 2 = F-100 high calorie. High calorie in Phase 1 risks refeeding syndrome.

label: SRNS — Tacrolimus trough
explanation: Tacrolimus trough 5–10 ng/mL in maintenance. Higher (10–15) in first 3 months post-transplant.
trap: Nephrotoxicity increases at high troughs. Monitor renal function and blood levels.

label: Hepatic Encephalopathy — Lactulose
explanation: Lactulose 0.5–1 mL/kg/dose 2–3× daily. Titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day.
trap: Too little = no ammonia reduction. Too much = dehydration. Titrate to 2–3 soft stools.

label: Hyponatremia — Severe
explanation: Chronic hyponatremia: max 8–10 mEq/L in 24h. Faster = osmotic demyelination syndrome.
trap: Rapid correction >12 mEq/L/24h causes locked-in syndrome. Max = 8–10.

label: Constipation — Disimpaction
explanation: Disimpaction: PEG 3350 1–1.5 g/kg/day for 3–6 days. Maintenance: 0.4–0.8 g/kg/day.
trap: Maintenance dose is NOT for disimpaction. Must disimpact first or therapy fails.

label: PKU — Target Phe
explanation: PKU target: 2–6 mg/dL in children. Adults: <10 mg/dL. Classic PKU >20 mg/dL at diagnosis.
trap: Target is 2–6 mg/dL in children. <2 is too restrictive; >10 is neurotoxic.

label: HBV Neonatal Prophylaxis
explanation: HBIG 0.5 mL IM within 12h + Hep B vaccine at different site. Preterm <2 kg needs 4 vaccine doses.
trap: HBIG = 0.5 mL. Must be at DIFFERENT site from vaccine. <12 hours, not 24.

label: Central DI — DDAVP
explanation: Central DI: Desmopressin intranasal 5–20 mcg BID. Monitor for hyponatremia.
trap: DDAVP only works in central DI. Nephrogenic DI needs thiazides + low-solute diet.

label: AKI — Fluid overload
explanation: AKI fluid overload: Furosemide 1–2 mg/kg IV. Dialysis indications = AEIOU.
trap: Do NOT use NSAIDs in AKI — they worsen renal perfusion.

label: CDH — Gentle ventilation
explanation: CDH: TV 4–6 mL/kg, PIP <25, permissive hypercapnia. No bag-mask — intubate immediately.
trap: High pressure ventilation worsens pulmonary hypertension in CDH. Gentle ventilation is critical.

label: HRS Type 1
explanation: Type 1 HRS: Terlipressin 0.01–0.04 mg/kg IV q4–6h + Albumin. First-line medical therapy.
trap: Monitor for ischemia: chest/abdominal pain, arrhythmias, digital gangrene.

label: Renal Transplant — Tacrolimus
explanation: Tacrolimus trough 5–10 ng/mL maintenance. 10–15 ng/mL in first 3 months post-transplant.
trap: Nephrotoxic, neurotoxic, diabetogenic. Monitor closely. No live vaccines post-transplant.

label: ACTH Deficiency
explanation: Secondary AI: Hydrocortisone 8–10 mg/m²/day PO divided TDS. Stress = triple dose.
trap: Secondary AI has NO hyperkalemia, NO hyperpigmentation — aldosterone preserved. Key differentiator from primary AI.

label: Growth Hormone Deficiency
explanation: GHD: rhGH 0.16–0.24 mg/kg/week SC divided 6–7 doses. Continue until near-final height.
trap: rhGH is dosed per WEEK, not per day. Monitor IGF-1 and thyroid function.

label: Obstructive Jaundice
explanation: Vitamin K 1–2 mg IM/IV before any procedure in cholestatic infants. Corrects coagulopathy from malabsorption.
trap: Always give vitamin K before liver biopsy or Kasai surgery in cholestatic infants.

label: Crigler-Najjar Type II
explanation: Type II: Phenobarbital 2–5 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg/day). Reduces bilirubin 25–50%. Type I has NO response.
trap: Phenobarbital response distinguishes Type I (NO response) from Type II (>25% reduction).

label: Coeliac Disease — Serology
explanation: Anti-tTG IgA >10× ULN + positive EMA + symptomatic = can diagnose without biopsy in selected cases.
trap: Must also check total IgA to rule out IgA deficiency (false negative tTG).

label: RTA Type I — Alkali
explanation: Type I RTA: K-citrate 1–2 mEq/kg/day. Type II needs 5–15 mEq/kg/day. Type IV: treat underlying cause.
trap: Type I = 1–2 mEq/kg/day. Type II = 5–15 mEq/kg/day. Do NOT give potassium in Type IV (worsens hyperkalemia).

label: Hirschsprung Disease — Biopsy
explanation: Rectal biopsy must sample ≥2 cm above dentate line. Below is physiologically aganglionic = false positive.
trap: Biopsying <2 cm = false positive. Must be ≥2 cm for accurate diagnosis.

label: Wilms Tumor — Pre-op
explanation: Wilms tumor absorbs vWF -> acquired vWD. Check coagulation before surgery. Also: NEVER biopsy pre-op (upstages to Stage III).
trap: Acquired vWD + no pre-op biopsy are both high-yield Wilms tumor management points.

label: Chronic Hepatitis B
explanation: Entecavir 0.015 mg/kg/day (max 0.5 mg/day) is first-line for chronic HBV in children. High barrier to resistance.
trap: Interferon is NOT first-line in children due to side effects. Entecavir or Tenofovir are preferred.

label: CH — Levothyroxine absorption
explanation: Soy formula, iron, and calcium reduce levothyroxine absorption. Give separately, >=4 hours apart.
trap: Soy + iron + calcium all REDUCE T4 absorption. A real exam trap for CH management.

label: SRNS — Cyclophosphamide
explanation: Cyclophosphamide 2 mg/kg/day × 8–12 weeks. Cumulative limit: 168 mg/kg to limit gonadotoxicity.
trap: Cumulative limit = 168 mg/kg. Exceeding this increases gonadotoxicity risk.

label: Hepatic Encephalopathy — Rifaximin
explanation: Rifaximin 10–20 mg/kg/day. Synergistic with lactulose. Superior to lactulose alone in preventing recurrence.
trap: Combination of lactulose + rifaximin is superior to lactulose alone — always mention both.
